speaker: interviewer
text: Frieda Aaron. And today is February 2, 19-- February 5, 1995. And you're at the end of your stay at Skarzysko.

speaker: interviewee
text: Skarzysko, yes. I want to mention something that I regard as being significant. I hope you will, too. When we first got to Skarzysko and we started working in the ammunition factory, we worked with Poles-- that is, Christians-- Aryans. And there was one Polish woman who took a kind of liking to us-- to the three of us. We always tried to stay together. And, in fact, initially, she gave us-- the first time we arrived there, she gave me some bread. And I didn't want to take it. I was still proud. I mean, it's so mad that I was proud to take the bread. I was starving. It was so absolutely idiotic. But, of course, the starvation was so excruciating, my teeth were loose. Not only mine, everybody else's. And my legs and my mother's legs were covered with sores-- terrible sores. Starvation causes this. My mother's head swelled up from starvation. You know, it was just indescribable. And, of course, later we took the bread. But then, progressively, we deteriorated because of the conditions. And when I came back from the hospital, I was fortunate to have gotten gloves. Actually, all of us were supposed to wear these gloves, because we were sorting bullet shells, and they were supposed-- we were supposed to wear gloves. I don't remember precisely why. But, of course, none were available. But somehow was able to find a pair of gloves. Either my mother found them for me or my sister. And it was essential that I have them, because I had still these running sores-- suppurating, oozing sores.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was sitting next to this Polish woman. I always worked next to her. And finally, she got sick and tired of me. She spit at me. You stink, you Jewish-- whatever. OK? So there was a total transformation. Initially, she had some pity. She never understood that when we were home, before the war, we're clean. She could not make the transition. I mean, even when we came from Majdanek-- a time during which we looked pretty awful-- she just could not imagine that we're human. So somehow I regard her as being dehumanized, not myself. I was never dehumanized. I always retained my humanity, even in Majdanek, during the selections, we were not dehumanized.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the beasts-- those SS-- they were the beast. They were bestialized. We're always human. We never forget who we were. I never forgot that I was my mother's daughter, that I was my sister's sister, and, above all, that I was my father's daughter. Because he always held my hand. He was always there for me. So how could-- how-- You know, the reason that I'm making such an issue of it is that people talk about our dehumanization, even survivors. But sometimes they don't understand. Some of them don't understand what it means. But if you retain your sense of self, if your self-hood is intact, then you are-- then those who try to destroy you are the beasts. They are the dehumanized. So I think this woman was dehumanized, too, because, ultimately, she got sick and tired of my--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't blame her. I used to take off my scarf, you know, that thing? And lice would fall on the table. But it was not my fault. I told her, you know, we're pretty children, my sister and I. And we did not choose to be the way we are now. All right. So much-- for Skarzysko, there's much more to tell. How long were you there? A few months?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were there about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit over a year, I think, and then were sent to this other camp, which was also an ammunition-- they produced ammunition at this other camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll spell it to you. C-Z-E-S-T-O-C-H-O-W-A. Czestochowa, if you can pronounce it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very similar to the one in Skarzysko. As a matter of fact, we were in a barracks. It was almost like a building. Anyway, that's not important.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is important is that we also had these rows of bunk beds. And they were joined not only in one row, but in two. In other words, adjacent rows. Am I making myself clear? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks here, barracks here. So it was, like, doubled. 'Barracks'-- excuse me. Bunk beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: The beds-- yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Bunk-- can't say 'barracks.' The bunk beds. And one day, this whole structure collapsed. So there were two sets of these double bunk beds and triple-- double, and triple. Double horizontally, and triple vertically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six levels.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. No, there were three levels, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you put them together--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm confused.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were on the floor. OK, there was-- this is one bunk bed, OK? Let me take a book.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is one bunk bed, and this is another bunk bed. OK? And then this is over here, here, here. So there were two in the width and scores of them in length. Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? So this structure collapsed. You know? These bunk beds. And many, many people were injured. That was in Czestochowa. We worked in an ammunition factory there, too. Now, there was a river. The name of the river is Warta. And if you want me to spell it for you, I'll be glad to. W-A-R-T-A. On the banks of that river, people were punished. If they did something that was not acceptable, they had to stand at the edge of the river and gaze into it, because they had no choice. And if they did not faint, they lived. And if they did, they fell into the river and often drowned. So this was a means of punishment. Here, too, there were no gas chambers, just, you know, shootings. My mother, sister, and I, again, tried to be inseparable. My cousins, also-- the two male cousins-- were transported to the same camp. And at one point, my mother really looked just absolutely terrible. And there was an SS man who ran the factory-- the plant. And he decided that he wanted to send my mother to the gas chamber. Not on camp-- the campgrounds-- because there wasn't any. But just to send her away. And my cousin intervened, because he was a jeweler. And he was making-- he was making jewelery for the Germans who were there. So he had certain privileges. And the next day, this SS man, we used to call him Totenkopf, which means 'death head.' You know, they used to wear these, uh-- death-- you know, the head-- the skeletal symbol-- so. So when he saw my mother, he was enraged, and he beat her terribly. But there was no other transport going. And then, of course, the starvation was pretty much the same. I mean, it's unspeakable. And then, we heard rumors that the Russians were not too far away and that the war was coming to an end. And we're pleading with God. We're praying to God to send bombs-- even if the bombs were to kill us. So every time we heard something that sounded like a bomb, we're very happy. Then they-- the Germans did not want to leave any-- any, um-- traces behind them. So they were going to transport us to Germany, in trains. So the trains were prepared for us. And we-- we-- I don't-- I think they put us into the trains, but I don't remember clearly. And we got out, or else they never put us into the trains. And then we stayed there a couple of days and realized something was strange. And we walked out of that place where they put us into the-- or were about to put us into the camps. And we were told the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: People, on camp. And-- and-- and the Germans, actually-- no, most of them fled. Most of them fled. Our own people told us. And we did not believe it. My mother, my sister, I, and other people did not believe that, because we thought it was a ruse. It was a lie-- that the Germans were waiting, poised with machine guns, to shoot at us. So we were afraid to leave. Ultimately, we realized that, indeed, the war was over. I mean, it wasn't over, because it was January 15, 1945. The war was still on. But, for us, we were sort of liberated. Now I object very strongly to the word 'liberation' because we were never liberated. There was no one to come to liberate us. They simply stumbled upon us. They found us. I'm talking about the Americans, the British, the French-- whoever fought in the army-- certainly the Russians-- were not charged with liberating camps. They just happened to have come upon them. And they were horrified. But it was in some way-- although the people who were in camps in Germany were in some way worse off, because the war lasted-- their liberation-- or 'release'-- let's call it 'release'-- from camps occurred later, in May, you know. So they were there longer. But as a re-- well, I don't want to compete with them. It was awful, both places. But at least the Americans took care of them. They gave them food. They gave them clothing. They tried-- those who were still alive, they tried to take to hospitals who needed hospitalization. With us, it was totally different. Totally different. The Russians just-- you know, they were there, and we walked out, in our rags.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still had that housecoat. And it was bitter cold. And you might wonder, how did I sustain the cold? Well, we didn't have any straw in the sacks. You know, they had these straw mattresses, but they were essentially sacks. So, since we didn't have straw, we used it as capes. And the blankets with which we covered ourselves, we use-- we used as capes. And our feet were wrapped in rags or paper, if we found any. OK? So we left-- ultimately, we did leave that camp. My two cousins fled. And I don't blame them. They didn't wait for us, because nobody knew what was happening, and they wanted to save their lives. We still thought that this is not the end of the misery and the torture of that inferno. So ultimately, we did walk out. Then there was no place to go. There were no shelters. There was no one. And you know what we were greeted with? Rocks, thrown by some of the Polish population. So this was our greeting, after leaving camp. And then, for days, we wandered the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was January. And, as I said, it's bitter cold. Ultimately, we found a building. It was a schoolhouse that was emptied of everything except for cabinets, you know, where you keep books. And we found refuge there. And the pipes-- all the pipes burst, because of the cold. You know, the building was not heated. And all the refuse from the sewers was all over the place. But it was frozen, fortunately. And the Russian soldiers were notorious rapers. You know, and they didn't care whom they raped. And they came into that schoolhouse. And my mother found one of those cabinets. It was almost like a coffin. They're very narrow. They're not different from the school cabinets here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the three of us slept there. You know? This was our bed, and we sort of huddled and cuddled up to each other, because it was so cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once, a Russian soldier came, totally drunk. And my mother spoke Russian. I told you, my mother was born in Russia. And he asked us whether we were men or women. My mother said, in a deep voice, 'men.' And I remember he chased a girl. Whether he caught her or not, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we stayed for some time. And we ate, literally, garbage. We were like-- you know the homeless people who eat from the garbage cans? And, in fact, the very day that we left the camp, we happened upon a very nice Polish woman-- very poor. She looked almost like a witch. She lived in, like in a storefront room. And she invited us to come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said she was going to kill a cat to feed us. And she was going to cook a cat, because she cooked cats all the time. So she told us. But I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so-- we're starving. I just cannot describe the starvation. I weighed about 50 pounds after liberation. I was, like-- you know, some of those ghosts that you see on television-- the skeleton? So was my mother, and my sister, but especially I. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is physically-- has a better constitution, although I'm a very healthy person. But she's really-- she's really extraordinary. I wish you could interview her, because she's really special.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we don't want to eat the cat, you know. And we left. We slept one night, and we left. And, finally, we found that schoolhouse. And there we stayed for some time. And then my sister and I-- oh, no--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I think some of the Jewish organizations began to form, and they received help from the United States and Poland. And they formed-- in fact, I'm writing a book about this period now, about the postwar period in Poland, essentially. Germany, too, but essentially in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you, at this point? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was still in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, geographically, is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's-- gee, I don't know, myself, I'm ashamed to admit. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know, exactly--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: -Where it is. It's a holy city for the Christians. There was a very important church there. Anyway, I can't place it. I can't visualize the map, for some peculiar reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the city was more or less intact--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --When you went there, except for this schoolhouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were in a camp. I mean, we were not in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you found your way to the schoolhouse, was it in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the city was not badly bombed, if that's what you are referring to. Remember, Warsaw was totally destroyed after the Polish uprising, on which I don't focus, not because I don't want to, but because, you know, my focus is a different one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, somehow, the Jewish organization somehow helped us to move to another town. Now, I don't remember any details of that. And we got to another town-- Kielce-- where there was a terrible pogrom, you may have heard. 40 people after the war were killed by the Poles-- survivors. It's a known-- it's a known fact. That's a notoriously-- I mean, it's a notorious fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the reason that we went there is that some-- that we knew that we had some relatives in that town. And we heard a rumor that some of them survived. And it's K-I-E-L-C-E. And so we went to that town. And I remember, we were riding in a train, open car, you know, and it was bitter cold. And literally, literally, my rear end froze to it. You know, because it gets very, very cold. And we stayed in that place, with Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll switch tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's actually an airplane.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll wait for the airplane to pass, so we can hear you. I thought it seemed a little soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Better hurry up. Yes. Well, finally we got to Kielce, and we're housed with Russian soldiers who were rather nice to us. And they shared food with us. But it was very scanty. The food certainly was not adequate to sustain us, you know, physically. There was a Russian couple. They were both officers. The woman, I think, was a physician. I'm not sure. And they wanted to adopt me. They were people perhaps in their 30s, maybe older. And, of course, it was a crazy idea. I would never leave my mother and sister. They wanted to take me to Russia-- to the Soviet Union. And perhaps if they wanted to take us all three, maybe-- the chances are we would have gone, you know, with them, simply because we didn't know what, you know-- much about what was happening in postwar communist Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, obviously, I didn't go. But I learned a little Russian, and I taught-- there was a soldier who taught me a little Russian. My hair begin to grow back. Already I had a little fuzz.

speaker: interviewee
text: But still were starving. So my sister and I took it upon ourselves to go to those German towns that were on the border of Poland and Silesia. And many people did it. Essentially, what we did-- we raided these places, because they were abandoned by the Germans. The Germans fled, and they left the homes-- their homes empty. And, actually, what we were looking for was food. But by the time we got there, there was no food to be found. And, in fact, en route, we were hiding with two other girls who were also-- who also embarked on this journey, you know, to find food. It was like an odyssey. And we find ourselves in an empty building. I don't know if it was a schoolhouse, too. I don't remember. And the Russian soldiers were abroad. They were all over the place, and we were terrified of being raped. But, fortunately, we were not. But anyway, they joined us, and we went to some of those border towns. And by the time we got there, there was hardly anything left. But we met a group of Israeli soldiers who were part of the British army. And they gave us some things. And, in fact, one of the soldiers listened to us as attentively as you do. Maybe not this fully. Not your attentiveness, but there wasn't all that much time. And he wanted to take us to Israel. But we couldn't go, because our mother was in Kielce. Just the two of us went-- my sister and I. So, obviously, we didn't go. And then we found the two cousins who left Czestochowa-- the two male cousins, who just disappeared. My mother and sister went to their town. And there were no trains, there was no transportation, so they walked for miles. Finally, they got to their town. But en route, they were-- the Poles-- they recognized us, not so much by our physiognomy, not because we looked Jewish, but because of our dress, because we were wearing rags. You know, they recognized us immediately. And they'd sic dogs on my mother and sister. But there was one who took them in. You know, they'd walked for days. Ultimately, they got to this town, you know, where my cousins lived. And then my younger cousin came and got me. And so we went to that town. And my uncle-- that is, my cousin's father, who was a brother of my father-- we have the same last name, Wakszlak. They had a house. They had a business. And they had a small house, you know, in which both the business and the apartment was located.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they came back, the Poles appropriated-- a Polish family appropriated the building to themselves. They would not return it to my cousins. But they gave them a pantry. OK? A room that was a pantry. It had one window. And in it, after I came to-- Dzialosyce is the name of the town. Do you want me to spell it? D-Z-I-A-L-O-S-Z-Y-C-E. OK? Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And their--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was their hometown--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Your cousins' hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And we're terrified of the Poles, because there was what was called the Armia Krajowa or the-- the-- it was a fascist organization. And, in fact, during the war, they killed a great many Jews who wanted to join them, to fight against the Germans. There was also another home army, and they were much more decent, much more cooperative. They happen to have been socialists. And these were fascists. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And it seems that the sons-- not it seems. We knew that the sons of the person who occupied the house, who appropriated the house to himself, belonged to that group-- you know, to the, um-- I can't think, in English, of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fascist group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of the fascist group-- Armia Krajowa. And my cousins were astute enough to know that something is bound to happen to us, you know. And so they knew-- See, they gave them that pantry-- that is, the Poles gave them that pantry-- because they knew that my uncle hid gold. He was also a goldsmith. The whole family were goldsmiths. So he hid some jewels and money. So they were kind of watching-- you know, spying on my cousins. But my cousins were more clever than they, and they did uncover the-- you know, they did find what their father hid in a different building. You know, it was like a barn or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had a sense that something terrible's going to happen. And we fled in the morning, one day. And that very night, they killed almost every Jew who was there. There were not too many who came back from the concentration camps. So they murdered them all-- or virtually all. OK? So then, we went to another town. And, since my-- Chorzow. C-H-O-R-Z-O-W. I think that's the way it's spelled. Could be spelled just with a Z. I don't remember. And I think it's just with a Z. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, my cousins-- because they were jewelers and because there was already some measure of justice, if you will, my cousins were given a business-- a store-- a jewelry store. It was just loaned to them-- that belonged to a German national, before, who fled. And his wife remained. And the store was--

speaker: interviewee
text: On top of the store was the apartment where that man lived, and this is where we lived, with that woman. OK? She had to share the apartment with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, the same Polish fascists, Armia Krajowa-- I'm trying to translate into English, and I can't think of it. Was bent on killing us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Well, probably other Jews, too, but they felt that they have access to the apartment, because of the German. Actually, she was Russian. The woman was Russian. She was married to a German national.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she bolted the door. And we were sure that was the end of us, you know? This was a form of pogrom, too. Not the group pogrom, but they were going to kill us. And, ultimately, they had to flee, because the Russian soldiers were sort of walking around.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- it was a very otherworldly place, altogether. I mean, the Russians came in, and they used to steal things from the store. And if I-- I was selling. I was the saleslady. And I didn't look particularly Jewish. People told me I looked German, about which I did not feel too complimented. But they really thought-- some of the Jewish people who came in didn't think that I was-- you know, my cousin had to tell them that I was his cousin. Anyway, ultimately, we had to flee from-- we wanted to go to Germany. And it was extremely difficult. First of all, we didn't have any papers. All of our papers were destroyed, so we couldn't prove anything. And the only way we could leave Poland-- they didn't want us there, but they would not let us go, either. So the only way we could do it is to claim that we're born in Austria. So my cousin went to Krakow, and he was able to produce false birth certificates, because we had nothing else. And, in his anxiety and excitement, he-- Well, first of all, we decided that we'll be a family-- a mother and four children-- and that we're all born in Austria. And so, when he made out these birth certificates, in his-- as I said, his tension, he wrote on one of them that my younger cousin was only three or four months older than I. The date that he gave-- one was born January, one was born in April or some such of the same year.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, you know, we were caught on the border of Poland and Czechoslovakia, where the Poles were. And they sent us back, so we had to go through the same thing, you know. And we stayed in some awful places. And my cousin went back to Krakow. And we couldn't go back in Chorzow, to that apartment, obviously. Anyway, I did tell you that the woman did not let them in, right? She bolted the door. She was really quite decent. And ultimately, we got other papers and were able to pass through the-- the searches and everything. What do you call it when you pass a border? There are the, uh-- the officials who are there, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they searched us thoroughly. You know, my mother had already a girdle. I already had a bra, which was a big deal. My hair grew back. You know, so we looked human. And we stayed in Czechoslovakia a few days. And then we smuggled into the-- ironically, into Germany. And the reason that we went to Germany was that the Americans were there. And the Americans let us pass. They kind of pretended they did not see us. And we went to a small town in Germany-- Hoff an der Saale. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that? H-O-F-F, A-N-- separately-- A-N-- small A-N-- Saale, capital S-A-A-L-E. It was close to the Czechoslovakian border. OK. And let's pause right there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --To switch the tapes.
